query: Redential complaints OR frustrating OR broken

results.title: How to handle resident complaints in a timely manner.
results.content:
Set a Routine: Schedule regular inspections of common areas, utility infrastructure, and key community assets. Address any issues immediately rather than waiting for residents to notice.
 Log Findings: Keep a record of inspection findings and act on them quickly, reducing the likelihood of complaints about things like broken amenities or neglected maintenance.

For more strategic maintenance guidance, check out our 4-Step Spring Cleaning Plan for Manufactured Housing Communities, which prioritizes safety, efficiency, and a welcoming atmosphere during seasonal preparations.

#### 4. Educate Residents on the Complaint Process

An informed resident is a less frustrated resident. Make it easy for residents to understand how to report issues and what they can expect in terms of resolution: [...] Clear Instructions: Post guidelines in common areas or include them in welcome packets and emails, detailing how to submit a complaint and what channels to use.
 Set Expectations: Let residents know the typical timeline for resolving different types of issues. This transparency helps manage expectations and reduces frustration.

By applying these tips, property managers can significantly improve complaint resolution times and build stronger relationships with their residents.

#### 5. Leveraging Software to Streamline Resident Complaints [...] #### 2. Improve Team Communication

Real-time communication among staff is key to resolving complaints quickly. Even simple tech tools like messaging apps (e.g., Slack, Microsoft Teams, or even group texts) can help:

 Collaboration: Property managers, maintenance staff, and office teams should be able to communicate instantly about ongoing complaints.
 Accountability: Use messaging apps to notify team members when a new complaint is submitted, allowing for faster response times and smoother coordination.

#### 3. Conduct Regular Proactive Inspections

Frequent property inspections help identify potential issues before they become tenant complaints. By staying ahead of problems, property managers can reduce the overall volume of complaints:

results.title: 10 Common Resident Complaints & How to Manage Them - Hales Property Management
results.content:
## 3. Unsafe conditions

Another common complaint is that the building is not safe. This could be due to a number of factors, such as broken security cameras or a lack of lighting in the parking garage.

It’s important to take these complaints seriously and address them immediately. By making sure the building is safe, you can put residents’ minds at ease and prevent accidents from happening.

## 4. Noisy neighbors

Noisy neighbors are another common issue that condo associations have to deal with. Whether it’s a party that’s going on late at night or someone playing music loudly during the day, this can be a major source of frustration for residents. [...] ## 6. Common area damage

Residents may also complain about damage to common areas, such as the lobby or gym. This can be due to a number of things, such as negligence or vandalism.

It’s important to address these complaints quickly. By taking care of the problem, you can prevent it from happening again in the future.

## 7. Broken amenities

If your building has amenities, such as a pool or gym, residents may complain if they’re not working properly. This could be due to a number of things, such as maintenance issues or a lack of funding.

It’s important to address these complaints in a timely manner. By taking care of the problem, you can keep residents happy and prevent them from going elsewhere.

## 8. Pet issues [...] ## 10. Billing issues

Finally, billing issues are also a common source of frustration for residents. This could be due to a mistake on the bill or a misunderstanding about the charges.

It’s important to have a system in place for dealing with billing issues. You should also train your staff on how to handle these types of complaints. By taking care of the problem, you can prevent it from happening again in the future.

These are just a few of the most common complaints that condo associations receive. By being prepared to handle them, you can keep your residents happy and prevent problems from getting out of hand.

If you need help in managing the operations of your building, reach out to us to request a proposal!

results.title: How To Overcome Distrust and Broken Credibility
results.content:
Don’t work against yourself. If you don’t do what you said you would do – over time you start working against yourself. If you don’t keep promises – you start working against yourself. If you state values but don’t act in alignment with them – you start working against yourself. If you don’t complete what you started – you work against yourself.

Don’t work against yourself. Keep your word.

Second Pillar: Cultivate Character

Character. A common colloquialism describes character as, “What you do when no one is watching.” Being a person of character is becoming someone who allows themselves to be led by guiding virtues. Such as integrity, generosity or kindness. [...] Skill in your profession may make leadership positions available to you. Ambition and assertiveness will, as well. But the credibility that comes with your professional skills will eventually fade if you don’t cultivate leadership skills. Ambition and assertiveness have value, but neither are great pathways to building credibility.

Leadership is a skill. There is both art and science behind what makes leadership work. The more proficient you become as a leader, the more credibility you’ll earn. Here are the twelve basic skills that I believe leaders need to cultivate:

1.      The ability listen well and understand underlying interests.

2.      The ability to ask good questions.

3.      The ability to communicate clearly and well in as many formats as possible. [...] Being “contrarian” is frequently advised to people like myself – people who are writing, trying to make a little splash, people who are looking to influence the way others think and act. Being contrarian is edgy. It catches people’s attention. Unfortunately, being contrarian for no purpose other than to get attention gets reduced to just being oppositional and cynical.

Now, being oppositional and cynical is viewed as a morally or intellectually superior perspective.

It isn’t.

The inability to create value, for others, is never morally or intellectually superior.

The Answer? We Need To Learn to Earn Credibility

Many people believe that credibility comes with a particular position or role or accomplishment. And it might. But only for a time. Then you need to earn it.

Illegal housing tears away at the fabric of the neighborhood .For several years the Police Department was receiving an increasing number of complaints associated with quality of life problems. Complaints were being received from neighborhood associations, private residents, local businesses and elected officials. These complaints were being generated from various parts of the city from numerous problem locations. Many of these problems were systemic of building and fire code violations, as well as overcrowding and illegal housing. Additionally, the demand placed by this overcrowding and illegal housing problem was straining and [...] to a large number of dwellings that are susceptible to abuse. The health of a city can be mirrored by a healthy housing stock. Conversely illegal housing unaddressed is synonymous to the theory of Broken Windows. Ignored, they lead to greater and greater problems. Illegal housing tears away at the fabric of the neighborhood. For several years the Police Department received an increasing number of complaints associated with quality of life problems. These complaints were generated from various and numerous parts of the city. Many were systemic of building and fire code violations, as well as overcrowding and illegal housing. The Department was utilizing a traditional approach in responding to these calls for service. It would service complaints that fell under their jurisdiction and all [...] Program, saving the State of New York $225,960 dollars. • Working with elected officials to develop State level legislation to prosecute offenders of the STAR Program. • Part 1 Crimes decreased 15% from 2008 – Lowest crime total since 1961 • Decreased number of complaints associated with quality of life problems • Improved the quality of housing stock, thereby reducing overall calls for service 6% reduction realized in first 18-24 month period 15.7% reduction projected for 24-36 month period (2010) • Built lasting partnerships, including city agencies, businesses, residents and elected officials • Program restored neighborhood pride and renewed resident cooperation There have been numerous times when a scheduled inspection conducted by the Quality of Life Task Force led to additional
